Hello everybody and welcome to Seven Figure Emails number three. This one is called the End Justice for All. If it's the first one you're watching for some reason, this is how it's going to go down. First we're going to give a little bit of a background on the template, how it came to be. Then we're going to dive into the persuasion principles. Really have a deep dive of what's at play here, why it works, and essentially why we send this out. And then we're going to look the template itself, including some questions you could ask yourself to really extract the best possible content. And then in the next video below this one we're going to look at an example from a launch we did. So that's what's in store, but first the background. So I am definitely, definitely, definitely not a Metallica fan, but this was the first song I remember listening to as a kid, End Justice for All. It was essentially the soundtrack to my third birthday party, thanks to my dad. Terrible parenting. I don't know why he would do that. But anyway, I was haunted by this song forever, had a little bit of PTSD. But while I'm borrowing the title from this song in my childhood trauma, trying to resolve it on this training session, this email is really about poor old Andy Dufresne, Shawshank Redemption, one of the best movies ever I refer to it so frequently when it comes to marketing, because there are so many hidden lessons within it. But let's back up for a sec. Last session, in case you didn't watch it, it was called the Simon Cowell qualifier. And that one was all about calling your prospect to a higher standard, a higher level of expectation for themselves of what they can achieve based on elements of exclusive empowerment, exclusive empowerment, sorry, based off things they have maybe achieved, based on maybe some qualitative aspects like their work ethic or things they're capable of, based off of their networks, based off of anything that exclusively empowers them to achieve a higher standard. That's what the Simon Cowell qualifier was all about. This is actually a perfect follow up because it signals and agitates the injustice between where they are and where they should be based off of those elements of exclusive empowerment. So pay attention to the language in here because it's so important should is not the same as could. One of them is so much stronger when it comes to marketing and persuasion. Could is essentially what you dream up and your passion planner on a Sunday afternoon with a low fat chai latte and bonny bear playing through your headphones. It's aspirational. It looks like this, which is a very regrettable photo shoot I did a few years ago. There's nothing written in that journal. I don't know why I did it. But yeah, I don't know, it happened. Anyway, should versus could. This is what could looks like. It's dreamy. There's nothing urgent about it. It's something you do on a Sunday afternoon. Whereas should is the stuff that keeps you up at night with a guttural clench that rivals that Metallica induced PTSD that my father left me with after my third birthday party and for higher conversions. And that's what this is really about. All these templates, we have to stop Bonnie bearing and start Metallica in very hard to say, but I think you follow. So no matter what you're selling, no matter what, even if it's a luxury item, you've got to market as low down on Maslow's pyramid as you possibly can. You know, self actualization and the steam needs happen only once you feel like you have your stuff together. And when was the last time you met anyone who actually feels like they have all their stuff together? Maybe from an outside perspective, it looks like they do. It looks like they have stuff figured out more than anyone else. But is that really their experience? Are they really chasing after those esteem needs and those self actualization needs? Maybe a very, very, very, very, very small percentage of your market actually is. Whereas safety needs are what people need to take care of right away. And this is where we place that emotional unrest caused by injustice between where they are and not where they could be could be a self actualization, but where they should be based off of who they are, what they've already achieved and what they're capable of that is the injustice we want to market and that exists lower on that pyramid. Cool. So what's that play here on how do we actually make this super practical in our email copy? Well, the first thing is we are going to be using injustice as the moment of highest tension. If you haven't taken 10x launches or seen another template where we break down the moment of highest tension, this is essentially the three dimensional actual moment in time where the pain that your product solve is most acute and urgent. So one example of that is when you get a tax bill in the mail and you open it up and you see that number piercing at your face and all of a sudden you have this guttural clench. So having to pay taxes isn't the pain. That's not a moment of highest tension. It sucks, but it's not a moment of highest tension. The moment of highest tension is when you get it back, you look at the number and it's actually higher than you thought it would be. So here we're using injustice as the moment of highest tension and the signaling of the injustice is really in the gap between how things are, how they're currently experiencing their life and how things should be and this is so much stronger than just pointing to pain alone. Let's prove it. What's more upsetting to you? A $50,000 tax bill, which is a basic pain or a $45,000 tax bill that should have been $35,000 if your accountant knew about the updated tax code. So the first one is you're paying more. The second one, you're paying less, but you should have paid even less. There's an injustice there. So one is a basic pain. One is an injustice and most people would find B a lot more upsetting than A even though the net effect isn't as bad. So this is the upset test. Use words like still and should to imply injustice. That's a little trick to know that you're actually marketing something that should be instead of could be. Actually use the word should. This was one of the highest performing stales pages I wrote. Look at the pre headline qualifier. This is literally the first thing people read. Important question for coaches, experts, course creators and online business owners who still don't have a profitable list, profitable email list. So within that one word still, there's an implied injustice as if they should have that list. It's not who could have a profitable list, which is like aspirational and fun and great, but higher on the scale of Maslow's hierarchy of needs. We are marketing that implied injustice. They should be there based off of of, you know, their expertise in their industry or the value they're putting out. That is the injustice that is going to lead to more urgent action. Persuasion principle number two redemption as the moment of highest pleasure. So once again, moment of highest pleasure is the opposite of the moment of highest tension. This is the vivid three dimensional moment where the benefits and the value that your product delivers is most present in your life. So this would be when your accountant, for example, fixes it and you get your new tax bill and you've seen that it's been lowered by $15,000. That would be an example of the moment of highest pleasure. But here, instead of it just being kind of any random joy or excitement, we are using redemption, the correction of that injustice, the removal of it and the restoration of how things should have been, which is often more powerful than a desired outcome on its own. And let's prove that with another movie reference. So what's more memorable? This movie blank check where this kid essentially gets a blank check and I think he writes a million dollars. I don't know why he didn't go for a billion. I mean, it probably would have bounced anyway. I mean, it should have bounced right from the beginning. That's such a flawed premise. Ironically written by one of the main screenwriting coaches, I think Sid Field wrote this one. Anyway, I digress. What's more memorable? Blank check or Andy Dufresne. In one movie, it's a kid who essentially has like total wish fulfillment, getting a million dollars and living the life versus Andy Dufresne. For two and a half hours, we watch this guy essentially go from being young and married and in love and loving life to aged, I think like 25 years and crawling through a pile of raw sewage just to get into this moment where he's getting drenched in the rain and probably going to get pneumonia and be really sick. Yet we celebrate that moment. That's one of the most memorable moments in film. He's actually worse off, but he's removed that injustice and that feels so good. That's what the redemption is. And that's why we want to use redemption as a moment of highest pleasure. So another test, the pleasure or relief test, what feels better a $30,000 tax bill or a tax bill that got reduced from 45 to 33 after your account had noticed a mistake and refiled. So think about that one for one second, which one feels better? Is it the lower net cost or is it the removal of an injustice and the relief you feel from no longer being screwed? A lot of people would say be. So this is all about redemption, feeling justified, another music reference for no apparent reason. Just let Justin look at you for a second longer and now we're gone. Okay, so persuasion principle number three, we invest time, money, energy to close the gap between this moment of highest tension, which is the injustice at the moment of highest pleasure, which is the redemption of it. Nothing is more urgent to us than when we're aware of an injustice and we have to close it. You know, we're hooked for two and a half hours just to see the reversal of this injustice where he ends up worse than he began it. That's an investment. We are willing to invest time to see the removal of an injustice and your audience is willing to invest money, time and attention to remove the indresses in their lives with your product. Cool. I mean, I wasted half a day to get a $5 refund on rotting cauliflower. I was so upset. I went to the grocery store. I was like, this organic, fancy place, which I didn't even really want to go to, but I did because we needed organic cauliflower for some reason for our two year old. I don't know. I can eat regular cauliflower, but once again, I digress. So cauliflower, come home from the store. Notice that it's all rotting. And I'm like, no way they're going to get away with this. Of course, my time is worth more than $5 an hour, but I had no choice. I had to invest more time and energy just to go back to the store for a negative net effect on my time. I had to go back there just to get a refund on this cauliflower. It's also why I wasted half a day with Apple Geniuses trying to get, I can't remember what it was. Yeah, my laptop keyboard wasn't working. And of course, I could have just, I mean, I think it was like a five year old MacBook Air already. It was probably worth not much. I could have probably bought a new one and I would have had to get one anyway, but I was just so enraged. I'm like, why is this keyboard not working? So I had to correct that injustice, even if it meant a negative net effect by wasting half a day working with these geniuses to try to get something fixed that needed to be replaced anyway. So key takeaway is stop treating desire or pleasure as something separate from pain. Start treating desire as the removal of injustice and pleasure is the moment immediately following it. It's that sheer relief of no longer having that injustice in your life. And justice is the ultimate emotional open loop. We literally can't rest until justice is restored. We must take action, even when it's not rational, like a head of broadened cauliflower. So some message mining questions. So remember, the injustice is based off of what they are exclusively empowered to strive towards how reality should be based off of things they've achieved, based off of actions they've taken or that they're willing to take, and based off of aspiration, what they expect from themselves. So these are some questions that are going to give you those elements of exclusive empowerment, which create the injustice. Where to send it? So this could be an invitation to a conversion event like the webinar I invited actually works really well as a follow up to the Simon Cowell qualifier that we looked at in the last session and like that one, it could also be a standalone sales email. So all that said, here's the template. Very simple. We're going to open up with that moment of highest tension, which is really this existential injustice based off of who they are and where they're at. And then we're going to agitate it with that stack of why. Why is this an injustice? And the why comes down to what they're exclusively empowered for, you know, actions, accomplishments, ambitions, past, present, future, explaining why that moment of highest tension shouldn't be happening for them. Once again, shouldn't. And then we're going to state that injustice in one clear, one clear sentence, using a should or still diagnose why it's happening. You know, we're really the trusted advisor that could tell them why this injustice is still present in their lives. And then a call to action framed as a way to remove that injustice. So I call this kind of the spoon that Andy Dufresne broke out of Shashank with we are giving them the tool to end that injustice and achieve that redemption. And then some fashion, fascinations around our product to support the call to action. So as you'll notice the fascination to really come late here, we're not trying to intrigue them or draw their interest and desire around painting up how great our product is, we're highlighting the injustice that is already happening in their lives and just using our product as the spoon to dig their way out. Fascinations just support the fact that this is indeed the spoon that corrects the injustice. And then a final call to action. So that's it. This is a super powerful template. Play it loud on your Metallica. You can play it loud on your Metallica. That doesn't even make sense. I don't even like this gift for this meme or whatever it is. It's just drawing up way too much PTSD. But anyway, that's it. We have a example walkthrough in the next video. Give that a watch and I will see you very, very soon. Thanks.
